nativeRange.habitat: Outer reef slopes, lagoon edges, and channel margins; typically forms aggregations 1–2 m above coral or rocky substrate at depths of 2–30 m (most commonly 5–20 m).

diet.notes: Primarily a zooplankton feeder in the wild; in captivity generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp, frozen copepods, frozen brine shrimp, and high-quality marine pellets. Tends to do well with multiple small feedings per day.

avoidWith: other male Cirrhilabrus in small tanks

summary: A colorful and generally peaceful fairy wrasse from the Indo-West Pacific, commonly regarded as reef-safe and well-suited to established reef aquariums of at least 200 L with ample open swimming space.
careNotes: Cirrhilabrus cyanopleura (Blueside Wrasse / Yellowflanked Fairy Wrasse) is distributed across the Indo-West Pacific from the Andaman Sea and Similan Islands through the Philippines, Indonesia, Papua New Guinea, Palau, and down to the Great Barrier Reef and Christmas Island; it has also been recorded from the Ryukyu Islands and Taiwan. In the wild it tends to form aggregations 1–2 m above reef structure along lagoon margins and outer reef slopes at depths of roughly 5–20 m. FishBase records a maximum standard length of 15 cm; BlueZoo Aquatics lists maximum size as 6 inches (~15.2 cm), placing both sources in good agreement. Males are markedly more colorful than females — the species exhibits clear sexual dimorphism typical of the genus, and males often intensify coloration during courtship displays. As with most Cirrhilabrus wrasses, this species is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite; females may transition to male in the absence of a dominant male. Best kept as a single male, or as a male introduced simultaneously with one or more females; multiple males in a small system have been known to conflict. A tight-fitting lid or euro-brace is strongly advised — fairy wrasses are documented jumpers, and open aquariums carry a real risk of escape. The species is commonly considered reef-safe and has not been widely reported to nip corals or ornamental invertebrates. Note: the trade name 'Yellow-Flanked Fairy Wrasse' is also applied by some retailers (including LiveAquaria) to Cirrhilabrus ryukyuensis (Ryukyu Wrasse), a distinct species from the Western Pacific with a more restricted range; buyers should verify the scientific name before purchase. Temperature data from FishBase: stated range 23–28°C; modeled preferred mean 28.2°C.
breedingNotes: Breeding in home aquaria has rarely been documented. The species is a protogynous hermaphrodite; dominant females may transition to male if no male is present. Distinct pairing behavior during spawning has been reported in the wild, with males performing elaborate color displays to court females.

primary.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
primary.notes: Product page confirmed scientific name as Cirrhilabrus cyanopleura; provided adult size (6 in), minimum tank size (50 gal), care level (beginner), temperament (peaceful), diet (carnivore), reef compatibility (yes), native range (Indo-Pacific), and escape-risk warning.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name, taxonomy (Labridae), maximum size (15.0 cm SL), native range (Indo-West Pacific: Rowley Shoals, Andaman Sea, Christmas Island, Ryukyu Islands, Taiwan, Philippines, PNG, Palau, Indonesia, GBR), temperature range (23–28°C, preferred mean 28.2°C), habitat (aggregations 1–2 m above reef/rocky bottom, lagoon edges, channel margins, outer reef slopes; depth 2–30 m, typically 5–20 m), feeding ecology (zooplankton feeder, trophic level 3.4), sexual dimorphism, and protogynous breeding behavior.

additional.name: LiveAquaria
additional.notes: Primary source URL (collection page) did not have a direct listing for C. cyanopleura. The LiveAquaria product page titled 'Yellow-Flanked Fairy Wrasse' resolves to Cirrhilabrus ryukyuensis, a different species — water parameters (72–78°F / 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12) and escape-risk warning from that page are referenced as supplementary context for the genus but not used as primary data for this entry. Cross-source role filled by FishBase and BlueZoo Aquatics.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive spawning is rarely documented for this species. Protogynous hermaphrodite; dominant female transitions to male in absence of a dominant male. Wild spawning involves distinct pairing and courtship color displays by males.
